county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Danbury was burned by the British in 1777, but most town records survived as they had been moved beforehand.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Connecticut's four original counties (1666). County government abolished 1960. Towns are the primary record-keeping units. Includes Gold Coast towns (Greenwich, Stamford, Darien) with strong New York connections. Bridgeport was a major industrial city attracting Italian, Hungarian, and Slovak immigrants. Contains the Fairfield Probate District and numerous sub-districts.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fairfield County's proximity to New York means many families crossed the state line — check Westchester County, NY records alongside CT town clerk files. The Bridgeport Public Library (Burroughs-Saden branch) holds extensive local history materials including city directories from 1857 and Bridgeport factory employment records. For pre-Revolutionary families, the Fairfield Museum and History Center has 17th-18th century town records. Greenwich Historical Society is strong on colonial-era deeds. The Pequot Library in Southport has Fairfield town proprietors' records. Danbury's 1777 burning by the British scattered some early records, but most critical volumes survived relocation to nearby towns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Town-level land records via individual town clerk offices; some available through Connecticut Town Clerk portal

source_profiles.source_name: Fairfield County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records filed at town level (Bridgeport, Danbury, Norwalk, Stamford, Greenwich, etc.). Connecticut records land at the town, not county, level.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival across all towns. Continuous from earliest settlement.

source_profiles.source_name: Fairfield Probate District Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate districts in Fairfield County include Bridgeport, Danbury, Fairfield, Greenwich, Newtown, Norwalk, Redding, Shelton, Stamford, Stratford, Trumbull, and Weston. Each district keeps separate records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival. FamilySearch has digitized many volumes through early 1900s.

source_profiles.source_name: Barbour Collection — Fairfield County Towns Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lucius Barnes Barbour abstractions of births, marriages, and deaths from town clerk records for all Fairfield County towns through approximately 1850.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Barbour abstractions are comprehensive; original town records also survive.

source_profiles.source_name: Bridgeport Immigration and Factory Records
source_profiles.repository: Bridgeport Public Library / Bridgeport History Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: City directories, factory employment records, and ethnic community records for Bridgeport's major immigrant groups (Italian, Hungarian, Slovak, Polish). Includes P.T. Barnum-era Bridgeport records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; some gaps in factory records.

source_profiles.source_name: Congregational Church Records — Fairfield County
source_profiles.repository: Connecticut State Library / Connecticut Conference UCC
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational (Puritan) church records including baptisms, marriages, deaths, and membership lists from founding-era churches in Fairfield, Stratford, Norwalk, and Stamford. CSL holds originals or microfilm for most congregations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Strong survival for Congregational records; some early volumes damaged but largely readable.

source_profiles.source_name: Connecticut Adjutant General Records — Fairfield County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Revolutionary War and Civil War service records, pension files, and militia rolls for Fairfield County residents. Includes records of the Danbury raid (1777) and local defense forces.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for state-level records; federal records at NARA.

source_profiles.source_name: Connecticut Digital Newspaper Project — Fairfield County
source_profiles.repository: Connecticut State Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized newspapers including the Bridgeport Standard, Danbury Times, Norwalk Gazette, and other county papers. Obituaries, court notices, and land sale announcements.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Growing digital collection; many papers available on microfilm at CSL.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Hartford's records are among the best preserved in New England.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Connecticut's four original counties (1666). Hartford is both the state capital and former county seat. Home to the Connecticut State Library (CSL), which is the central genealogical repository for the entire state. County government abolished 1960. The Hartford Probate District is one of the oldest in the state (est. 1666).
county_metadata.research_tips: Hartford County is the single most important county for CT genealogical research because the Connecticut State Library (CSL) on Capitol Avenue houses the Barbour Collection (vital records), Hale Collection (cemetery/newspaper abstracts), and the state's entire probate and land record microfilm holdings. Researchers should start at CSL even for other counties. The Connecticut Historical Society on Elizabeth Street has manuscript collections, account books, and family papers for Hartford-area families dating to the 1630s. Hartford's insurance industry generated extensive employment records. For African American research, the Connecticut Emancipation records (gradual abolition starting 1784) are at CSL. The Wadsworth Atheneum archives hold colonial-era civic records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Hartford town clerk records online; other towns vary

source_profiles.source_name: Hartford County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from Hartford, Windsor, Wethersfield (the three founding towns), and all other Hartford County towns. Filed at town level per CT practice.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding survival — Hartford town records from 1636 are intact.

source_profiles.source_name: Hartford Probate District Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hartford Probate District (est. 1666) plus subdivided districts: Avon, Berlin, Bristol, Burlington, Canton, East Granby, East Hartford, East Windsor, Enfield, Farmington, Glastonbury, Granby, Manchester, Marlborough, New Britain, Newington, Plainville, Rocky Hill, Simsbury, South Windsor, Southington, Suffield, West Hartford, Wethersfield, Windsor, Windsor Locks.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent; microfilmed extensively by CSL and FamilySearch.

source_profiles.source_name: Hale Collection — Hartford County Cemetery and Death Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Charles R. Hale's monumental collection of cemetery inscriptions and newspaper death/marriage notices for Hartford County and statewide. Over 2 million entries covering all CT towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete manuscript collection at CSL; partially indexed.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census Records — Hartford County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Hartford County, 1790-1950. Hartford was one of the largest cities in CT and well-enumerated. CT also had a state census in 1917.
source_profiles.survival_notes: All federal censuses survive for Hartford County; 1890 census lost nationally.

source_profiles.source_name: Hartford County Superior Court Naturalization Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization declarations and petitions filed in Hartford County/District courts. Significant volume due to insurance industry immigration. CSL has indexes; originals split between CSL and NARA Boston.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; some early records on fragile paper.

source_profiles.source_name: Hartford Town Tax Lists and Rate Books
source_profiles.repository: Connecticut Historical Society / Hartford History Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial and early republic tax assessment lists for Hartford and surrounding towns. Useful for tracking property ownership before systematic deed indexing. Connecticut Historical Society holds manuscript tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial survival for earliest years; good from 1700 forward.

source_profiles.source_name: Connecticut Gradual Emancipation Records — Hartford Area
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of the gradual abolition of slavery under the 1784 act. Town clerk registrations of enslaved persons and those born into gradual freedom. Hartford County had the second-largest enslaved population in CT after Fairfield.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Fragmentary — town-level compliance varied. Best records in Hartford, Windsor, and Wethersfield.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Litchfield's rural character preserved records well.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1751 from portions of Hartford and Fairfield counties. Northwestern CT — the most rural county. County government abolished 1960. Contains 26 towns. The Litchfield Hills region was settled later than coastal CT. The Litchfield Law School (1774-1833), the first in America, attracted students from across the nation — its records are genealogically valuable.
county_metadata.research_tips: Litchfield County's rural, agricultural character means church records and town clerk vital records are often the only documentation for 18th-19th century families. The Litchfield Historical Society holds the records of the Litchfield Law School (1774-1833) and Litchfield Female Academy (1792-1833), both nationally significant — student rosters connect families across multiple states. Many Litchfield families migrated to the Western Reserve (Ohio) and Vermont in the late 1700s, so check outbound migration patterns. The town of Sharon has excellent Congregational church records from 1740. Torrington Historical Society documents the brass industry workers. Pre-1751 records split between Hartford and Fairfield counties depending on town location.

source_profiles.source_name: Litchfield County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 26 Litchfield County towns including Litchfield, Torrington, New Milford, Salisbury, Sharon, and others. Town clerk offices are the primary repositories.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival across all towns.

source_profiles.source_name: Barbour Collection — Litchfield County Towns Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Barbour abstractions of marriages, births, and deaths for all Litchfield County towns. Particularly valuable for tracking western CT family formation patterns and migration to Ohio's Western Reserve.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Comprehensive abstractions; original town records survive at town clerk offices.

source_profiles.source_name: Congregational and Episcopal Church Records — Litchfield County
source_profiles.repository: Connecticut State Library / Litchfield Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records from Congregational churches across the county's 26 towns plus Episcopal church records from the Seabury heritage. Litchfield's First Church of Christ records are particularly well-preserved from 1721.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good to excellent survival; CSL holds microfilm copies of most parish registers.

source_profiles.source_name: Litchfield Law School Student Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Student enrollment records from the first law school in America (Tapping Reeve). Over 1,000 students from across the nation. Genealogically valuable for identifying family networks and origins of students who later settled throughout the frontier.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved; digitization project ongoing.

source_profiles.source_name: Hale Collection — Litchfield County Cemetery Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Charles R. Hale cemetery inscription transcriptions for Litchfield County cemeteries. Includes many rural burying grounds with stones dating to the 1720s.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Hale transcriptions are complete; some original stones now unreadable, making this collection irreplaceable.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census Records — Litchfield County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for all Litchfield County towns. Census is especially important for tracking the county's significant population decline 1810-1850 as families migrated to Ohio and New York.
source_profiles.survival_notes: All federal censuses survive; 1890 lost nationally.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from portions of Hartford and New London counties. Smallest CT county by area. Middletown is the county seat and was a major Connecticut River port in the 18th century. County government abolished 1960. Contains 15 towns. The Connecticut River towns (Essex, Old Saybrook, Deep River, Chester) have strong maritime heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Middlesex County's Connecticut River towns — especially Essex, Deep River, Chester, and Old Saybrook — have rich maritime records. The Connecticut River Museum in Essex holds ship logs, crew manifests, and shipbuilding records essential for tracing seafaring families. Middletown was the largest and wealthiest city in CT in the 1750s; Wesleyan University's Special Collections hold significant Middletown family papers and account books. The Russell Library in Middletown has Middlesex County historical society records and local newspapers. For the African American community, Middletown's 'Beman Triangle' neighborhood records (1830s-1960s) document one of CT's oldest Black communities. Pre-1785 records are split between Hartford and New London counties by town.

source_profiles.source_name: Middlesex County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 15 Middlesex County towns. Middletown records from 1646 (originally part of Hartford). Deep River, Essex, and Old Saybrook have records tied to maritime property.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival.

source_profiles.source_name: Middlesex Probate District Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate districts covering Middlesex County towns: Middletown (est. 1752), Chatham/Portland, Clinton, Durham, East Haddam, Haddam, Killingworth, Old Saybrook. Inventories and distributions detail 18th-century Connecticut River merchant estates.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved; extensive FamilySearch digitization.

source_profiles.source_name: Barbour Collection — Middlesex County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Barbour abstractions of vital records for all Middlesex County towns. Middletown records are particularly detailed due to the town's importance as a colonial port.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Comprehensive; original records also survive.

source_profiles.source_name: Connecticut River Maritime and Naval Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Ship logs, crew manifests, and privateering records from Connecticut River ports. Essex was a major shipbuilding center — the British burned the town's fleet in 1814. Naval service records for local seamen in Revolutionary War and War of 1812.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — 1814 British attack on Essex destroyed some records along with ships.

source_profiles.source_name: Middletown Press and Middlesex County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Russell Library, Middletown / Connecticut State Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers including the Middlesex Gazette (1785) and Middletown Press. Obituaries, probate notices, and ship arrival announcements for Connecticut River ports.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival on microfilm at CSL and Russell Library.

source_profiles.source_name: First Church of Christ Records — Middletown
source_profiles.coverage_notes: First Church of Christ (Congregational) in Middletown — one of the oldest in CT. Baptismal, marriage, and death records. Wesleyan University Special Collections also holds related family papers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent for church records; some early volumes fragile.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county (from New Haven Colony, merged 1665)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. New Haven Colony records (1638-1665) survive at CSL and NHCHS.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Connecticut's four original counties (1666). New Haven was previously an independent colony (1638-1665) with its own governance before merger with Connecticut Colony. County government abolished 1960. Yale University (est. 1701) is a major research institution. Waterbury in the northern part was a major brass manufacturing center attracting Italian and Irish immigrants.
county_metadata.research_tips: New Haven County benefits from two extraordinary repositories: the Connecticut State Library and Yale University's Manuscripts and Archives (Sterling Memorial Library). Yale holds significant New Haven Colony records, diaries, correspondence, and family papers — search the Yale Finding Aids Database (YFAD) before visiting. The New Haven Museum (formerly NHCHS) on Whitney Avenue has New Haven Colony court records (1638-1665), which predate the county system and are among the oldest English-language court records in America. For Waterbury industrial families, the Mattatuck Museum has brass industry employment records and ethnic community documentation. The Knights of Columbus (founded 1882 in New Haven) Supreme Archives holds membership records valuable for Catholic immigrant genealogy. New Haven's Grove Street Cemetery (1797) has a published register.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Town-level records; New Haven earliest from colony period

source_profiles.source_name: New Haven County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from New Haven (from colony period 1638), Milford, Guilford, Branford, Wallingford, Waterbury, and other towns. Town-level filing per CT practice.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding — New Haven Colony records from 1638 are intact.

source_profiles.source_name: New Haven Colony Court Records (1638-1665)
source_profiles.repository: New Haven Museum / Connecticut State Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of the independent New Haven Colony's courts — among the oldest English-language court records in America. Include criminal proceedings, civil disputes, land distributions, and the famous 'phantom ship' inquiry of 1647. Published as 'Records of the Colony and Plantation of New Haven' (Hoadly, 1857).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — published transcriptions supplement originals.

source_profiles.source_name: New Haven Probate District Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate districts in New Haven County: New Haven, Cheshire, Derby, Guilford, Meriden, Milford, Naugatuck, North Branford, North Haven, Oxford, Wallingford, Waterbury, Wolcott. Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival; many volumes digitized by FamilySearch.

source_profiles.source_name: Waterbury Brass Industry and Immigration Records
source_profiles.repository: Mattatuck Museum / Silas Bronson Library, Waterbury
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment records and community documentation from Waterbury's brass manufacturing industry. Major immigrant groups: Italian (especially from Pontelandolfo), Irish, Lithuanian, and French Canadian. Mattatuck Museum holds company records; Silas Bronson Library has city directories and naturalization indexes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival for museum-held materials; some factory records incomplete.

source_profiles.source_name: Grove Street Cemetery Register — New Haven
source_profiles.repository: Grove Street Cemetery / New Haven Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published register of burials at Grove Street Cemetery (est. 1797), one of the first chartered burial grounds in the US. Includes Yale faculty, Civil War veterans, and prominent New Haven families. Eli Whitney, Noah Webster, and Charles Goodyear are buried here.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete records; published register available.

source_profiles.source_name: Barbour Collection — New Haven County Towns Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Barbour vital record abstractions for all New Haven County towns through approximately 1850. Includes records from the New Haven Colony period (1638-1665).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Comprehensive abstractions; originals survive at town clerk offices.

source_profiles.source_name: Yale University Divinity School — Connecticut Church Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Extensive collection of Connecticut church records deposited at Yale Divinity Library, including Congregational, Episcopal, and Baptist records from New Haven County and beyond. Baptismal registers, membership rolls, and session minutes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved in climate-controlled repository.

source_profiles.source_name: New Haven Town Rate Books and Tax Lists
source_profiles.repository: New Haven Museum / Connecticut State Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial-era and early republic tax lists for New Haven and surrounding towns. Document property ownership, livestock, and assessable personal property.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial for earliest years; good from 1700 forward.

county_metadata.record_losses: New London was burned by Benedict Arnold's raiding force in September 1781, destroying much of the town. However, most vital and land records had been secured beforehand and survived.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Connecticut's four original counties (1666). Southeastern CT with Long Island Sound coastline. County government abolished 1960. New London was a major whaling port and later home to the U.S. Coast Guard Academy and the Naval Submarine Base at Groton. The Mashantucket Pequot and Mohegan tribal reservations are in this county. Mystic Seaport is a major maritime heritage site.
county_metadata.research_tips: New London County is essential for maritime genealogy in New England. Mystic Seaport Museum's G.W. Blunt White Library is the premier American maritime research collection — ship logs, crew lists, whaling voyage records, and shipyard employment records document New London and Stonington whaling families from the 1780s to 1920s. The New London County Historical Society has extensive manuscript collections for local families. For Mashantucket Pequot and Mohegan genealogy, tribal records and the Mashantucket Pequot Museum & Research Center offer specialized assistance. Norwich was a significant early CT settlement — check the Otis Library for local history. The Lyman Allyn Art Museum in New London has Winthrop family papers. Benedict Arnold's 1781 burning of New London affected some records, but critical town clerk volumes survived relocation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Town-level land records; New London and Norwich earliest

source_profiles.source_name: New London County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from New London, Norwich, Groton, Stonington, Lyme, and other county towns. New London and Norwich records from the 1640s. Maritime-related property (wharves, warehouses) documented alongside residential land.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival despite 1781 burning — critical records were evacuated.

source_profiles.source_name: Barbour Collection — New London County Towns Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Barbour vital record abstractions for all New London County towns through approximately 1850. Includes Pequot War-era records from the earliest settlements.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Comprehensive abstractions; originals survive.

source_profiles.source_name: Mystic Seaport — Maritime and Naval Service Records
source_profiles.repository: G.W. Blunt White Library, Mystic Seaport
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Comprehensive collection of maritime records: ship logs, crew manifests, whaling voyages, privateering records, and Naval service documents. New London was the third-largest American whaling port. Also covers the U.S. Revenue Cutter Service (predecessor to Coast Guard) records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — Mystic Seaport is one of the best-preserved maritime collections in America.

source_profiles.source_name: New London Probate District Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate districts in New London County: New London, Colchester, East Lyme, Groton, Ledyard, Montville, Norwich, Salem, Stonington, Voluntown, Waterford, and others. Estate inventories detail maritime wealth and whaling investments.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census Records — New London County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for all New London County towns 1790-1950. The 1850-1870 censuses capture the peak whaling era and early submarine base development at Groton.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete survival except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: First Congregational Church Records — Norwich and Stonington
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Early Congregational church records from Norwich (est. 1660) and Stonington (est. 1674). Include baptisms, marriages, deaths, and disciplinary proceedings. Norwich records are among the most complete colonial-era church registers in CT.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved; microfilm at CSL.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from portions of Hartford and Windham counties. Northeastern CT, largely rural. County government abolished 1960. Contains 13 towns. The University of Connecticut in Storrs (Mansfield) is a major research institution. Tolland was the county seat with a well-preserved green and historic jail museum.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tolland County's most significant genealogical resource is the University of Connecticut's Archives & Special Collections at the Thomas J. Dodd Research Center in Storrs. UConn holds Connecticut town records on microfilm, manuscript collections for northeastern CT families, and the Alternative Press Collection. The Tolland Historical Society maintains the Old Tolland County Jail museum and local genealogical files. For Stafford Springs and Rockville (Vernon), textile mill employment records document 19th-century immigrant workers (Irish, French Canadian, Polish). Pre-1785 records split between Hartford and Windham counties depending on town location. The town of Coventry has Nathan Hale birthplace records. Bolton, Andover, and Columbia town clerks hold records from the 1720s.

source_profiles.source_name: Tolland County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 13 Tolland County towns: Tolland, Bolton, Andover, Coventry, Columbia, Ellington, Hebron, Mansfield, Somers, Stafford, Union, Vernon, and Willington. Filed at town clerk offices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival across all towns.

source_profiles.source_name: Barbour Collection — Tolland County Towns Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Barbour vital record abstractions (births, marriages, deaths) for all Tolland County towns through approximately 1850.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Comprehensive abstractions; original town records also survive.

source_profiles.source_name: Tolland-area Probate District Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate districts: Stafford (est. 1759), Ellington, Tolland, Coventry, Hebron, Bolton, Mansfield, and others. Wills, estate inventories, and guardianship records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; FamilySearch has digitized many volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census Records — Tolland County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for all Tolland County towns. Useful for tracking the shift from agriculture to textile manufacturing in towns like Stafford Springs and Rockville (Vernon).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Revolutionary War and Civil War Records — Tolland County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Nathan Hale was born in Coventry — local militia and Continental Army service records are strong for northeastern CT. Civil War regimental records for Tolland County enlistees. Pension files and bounty land applications.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-level records complete; federal records at NARA.

source_profiles.source_name: Tolland County Newspapers — Rockville Journal & Stafford Press
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers from Vernon/Rockville and Stafford Springs areas. Obituaries, marriage notices, mill worker news, and legal notices. UConn Libraries also hold some Tolland County periodicals.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival on microfilm; partial digitization via Chronicling America.

source_profiles.source_name: Hale Collection — Tolland County Cemetery Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hale cemetery transcriptions for Tolland County burying grounds. Many small rural cemeteries with early 18th-century stones.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Hale transcriptions complete; some original stones now weathered.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1726 from portions of Hartford and New London counties. Northeastern CT, often called 'The Quiet Corner.' County government abolished 1960. Contains 15 towns. The county seat historically moved between Windham, Brooklyn, and eventually Putnam. Largely agricultural with 19th-century thread mills in Willimantic (Windham) and Putnam.
county_metadata.research_tips: Windham County is CT's 'Quiet Corner' and the least-researched county — which means opportunities for original discovery. The Windham Textile and History Museum (in the former Willimantic Thread Company mill) documents the textile workers who powered CT's thread industry — immigrant records for French Canadian, Irish, Polish, and Puerto Rican workers. The Connecticut Eastern Regional Tourism District encompasses Windham County. Pomfret and Woodstock have well-preserved Congregational church records from the 1690s-1700s. The Brooklyn Historical Society has records from when Brooklyn was the county seat. For the Quinebaug Valley towns, check the Last Green Valley heritage corridor records. Pre-1726 records split between Hartford and New London counties. The Killingly Historical Society documents the early English and later French Canadian settlement of northeastern CT.

source_profiles.source_name: Windham County Town Clerk Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 15 Windham County towns: Windham, Ashford, Brooklyn, Canterbury, Chaplin, Eastford, Hampton, Killingly, Plainfield, Pomfret, Putnam, Scotland, Sterling, Thompson, and Woodstock.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival.

source_profiles.source_name: Barbour Collection — Windham County Towns Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Barbour vital record abstractions for all Windham County towns through approximately 1850. Especially useful for early Woodstock and Pomfret families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Comprehensive abstractions; original town records survive.

source_profiles.source_name: Windham-area Probate District Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate districts: Windham (est. 1719), Pomfret, Plainfield, Canterbury, Thompson, Brooklyn, Killingly, and others. Estate inventories are especially detailed for early farming families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; FamilySearch digitization ongoing.

source_profiles.source_name: Congregational Church Records — Windham County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational church records from Pomfret (est. 1690s), Woodstock, Canterbury, Windham, and other towns. These churches were the social center of early communities; records include baptisms, marriages, deaths, and church disciplinary actions. Canterbury's Prudence Crandall school records (1832-1834) document early African American education.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; CSL holds microfilm for most parishes.

source_profiles.source_name: Willimantic Thread Mill and French Canadian Worker Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment records and community documentation from the American Thread Company mill in Willimantic. Significant French Canadian immigration records. The museum also documents Irish, Polish, and later Puerto Rican workers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — company records are incomplete but supplemented by town vital records and church records.

source_profiles.source_name: Hale Collection — Windham County Cemetery Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hale cemetery transcriptions for Windham County burying grounds. Many early stones in Pomfret, Woodstock, and Canterbury cemeteries with dates from the early 1700s.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Hale transcriptions are complete for all surveyed grounds; some original stones deteriorated.

source_profiles.source_name: Windham County Town Tax Lists
source_profiles.repository: Connecticut State Library / town clerk offices
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town tax assessment lists documenting property owners, livestock, and personal property across Windham County towns. Useful for tracking landholding patterns in the agricultural 'Quiet Corner.'
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial survival — varies by town. Best for Windham, Pomfret, and Woodstock.
